title: Will Q2 earnings season be a sell-the-news catalyst for AI stocks?

Happy Tuesday out there, team 42, it's
your skipper here at Darius Developers
on our macro minute for Tuesday, July
7th, 2026. As always, we start with the
executive summary from today's lead-off
morning note, so let's dive right in.
Today's key macro question is, will the
Q2 earning season be a sell-the-news
catalyst for AI stocks? The short answer
is the risk of this transitory bearish
outcome is much higher than consensus
currently expects.
In terms of analytical nuance, Samsung's
Q2 results failed to satisfy lofty
buy-side expectations, denting a stock
up 150% year-to-date. Samsung, the top
memory maker by value, fell as much as
11% in Seoul, tripping a brief KOSPI
circuit breaker prior to closing down
9%. Quarterly profit surged 19-fold
to 89.4 trillion won or $59 billion,
yet it still underwhelmed investors.
Revenue doubled to a 171 trillion won or
113 billion dollars. The CBOE Nasdaq
Volatility Index tied to the Nasdaq 100
has climbed to near 27. That is the
highest since 2002 relative to the CBOE
VIX Index on the S&P 500. Correlation
marks another divergence as well. Nasdaq
100 names are now moving more in sync
than S&P 500 constituents. This The
expansion of both realized and relative
volatility in the AI trade reduces
balance sheet capacity to fund related
trades across the global buy-side. This
increases the risk that Q2 earning
season is a sell-the-news catalyst for
several popular AI stocks. All told, our
macro weather model is still generating
a bullish outlook for stocks, gold,
Bitcoin, and commodities, which
indicates a high probability of
sustaining a risk-on market regime.
Driving this condition are meaningful
tailwinds in the monetary policy and
liquidity cycles, as well as the modest
tailwind in the fiscal policy cycle. If,
however, the Fed, quote, play action
passes to set up the run, then the
monetary policy and liquidity cycles
will deteriorate into transitory
headwinds and trigger summer 1998 style
market dynamics, i.e. a deep dip that
should eventually be bought. If the Fed
quote looks through inflation, then the
risk-on market regime that Kiss and Dr.
Moe have been positioned for since April
11th will continue alongside their still
bullish positioning. As always, I'll
wrap up with the question from our
community. This one's titled Golden
Ratio in margin accounts. Says Mother
Nature strikes again. Says four peaks
can be recognized in margin account
highs, 2000, 2008, 2021, and perhaps
now. If you take the golden ratio of
2000, you get the 2008 peak. If you take
the golden ratio of 2008, you get the
2021 peak. If you take the golden ratio
of 2021, you get the levels now. So,
let's kind of zoom in on this chart to
give you guys a better indication of
that. So, this is a chart from from one
of our members here who posted in in our
community chat. Excellent analysis,
really really really really thoughtful.
The So, this is the peak in margin debt
here in in in 2000 in 2000, and then it
rallied to basically the golden ratio
1.618
to the peak in 2008, and then it rallied
from there to that golden ratio to the
peak
the 1.618 peak here in back in 2021, and
then we've since rallied to about the
golden ratio relative to that 2021 high.
So,
in our opinion, this is, you know, it's
highly supportive of our view that the
AI capex bubble is likely to conclude
with the secular bear market for a
variety of reasons, domestic political
reasons, geopolitical reasons, just from
a from a expectations and positioning
cycle
reasons. You know, everyone's going to
essentially have one trade on by the at
the end of this at the end of this this
build-out, and so ultimately there's
going to be nobody on the other side of
the trade when investors start to take
profits and book gains. There's a lot of
other, you know, a lot of other
fundamental dynamics that we've
embellished upon in our macro scouting
reports and and throughout our around
the horn weekly around the horn
presentations. So, check those out for
more details on that. But, the key
takeaway I'll say here is this is this
is outstanding Y axis analysis. What's
missing here is the X axis. The X axis
is what Kissel and Doctor Mo solve for
in terms of keeping you on the right
side of market risk throughout
regardless of we're in a risk-on market
regime or when a risk-off or in a
risk-off market regime. So, you know,
analysis like this, you know, however
thoughtful and it is incredibly
thoughtful and and really interesting,
it doesn't tell you when the market has
peaked. It doesn't, you know, so you
have to be be cognizant of that that,
you know, there's still an X axis
component here that you have to respect
in terms of understanding the evolution
of the six key macro cycles and the
distribution of probable economic policy
market outcomes that that that those
cycles are combining to to signal. So,
all right. So, we're going to wrap up
there. Darius Dale here presenting our
macro minute for Tuesday, July 7th,
2026. Everyone have a wonderful day out
there. Best of luck. We'll catch you
back here tomorrow. Cheers.
